era_context: The early 7th century CE is marked by the collapse of the Sui Dynasty in China and the founding of the Tang Dynasty, the continuation of the Byzantine-Sassanid Wars, the rise of Islam in Arabia (though not yet a major political force), and the height of Classic Maya civilization. This is a period of dynastic transition, military conflict, and religious ferment across Eurasia.

events.title: Tang Dynasty founded; collapse of Sui Dynasty
events.description: The Sui Dynasty (581–618 CE), which had unified China after centuries of division, collapses due to military overextension, failed campaigns in Korea, and widespread peasant rebellions. Li Yuan, a Sui general, declares himself emperor and founds the Tang Dynasty in 618 CE. The Tang will become one of China's greatest dynasties, lasting until 907 CE. The transition from Sui to Tang marks a major turning point in Chinese history, with the Tang establishing new administrative systems, military structures, and cultural policies that will define the medieval period.
key_figures: Emperor Yang of Sui (r. 604–618 CE, deposed)
Li Yuan (Emperor Gaozu of Tang, r. 618–626 CE)

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang, compiled 945 CE)
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang, compiled 1060 CE)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government, compiled 1084 CE)

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Tang Dynasty in 618 CE is well-attested in Chinese historical sources. The date is consistent across multiple sources.
cross_references: Marks the end of the Sui Dynasty (581–618 CE)
Leads to 626-001 (Li Shimin's coup and accession as Emperor Taizong)

events.title: Byzantine-Sassanid War: Sassanid forces continue offensive; capture of Antioch
events.description: Sassanid Persian forces under Khosrow II continue their military offensive against the Byzantine Empire. Antioch, one of the major cities of the Byzantine Empire and a center of Christian theology, is captured by Sassanid forces. This represents a major blow to Byzantine power in the eastern Mediterranean. The Sassanids are at the height of their territorial expansion, having conquered much of the Levant, Egypt, and parts of Anatolia.
events.certainty_note: The Sassanid capture of Antioch is attested in Byzantine and Armenian sources, though the precise date (618 CE vs. 613 CE) is debated by modern scholars. Some sources place the capture earlier in the Sassanid offensive.

events.title: Harsha's reign: Consolidation of North Indian empire
events.description: Harsha (r. 606–647 CE) continues his military campaigns and consolidation of North Indian territories. By 618 CE, he has established a vast empire spanning much of northern India. His reign is marked by administrative reforms, military campaigns, and patronage of Buddhism and Sanskrit literature.
key_figures: Harsha (Harshavardhan, Emperor of North India, r. 606–647 CE)
events.certainty_note: Harsha's reign is well-attested in Sanskrit sources, though specific events in 618 CE are not uniquely documented.

events.title: Classic Maya civilization: Political competition and monument construction
events.description: The Classic Maya civilization continues its period of political competition, warfare, and cultural flourishing. Major Maya centers are constructing stelae, altars, and pyramids. Epigraphic evidence documents dynastic conflicts and ritual activities. The period from 600–650 CE is marked by significant monument construction and political activity.
events.certainty_note: Maya chronology is based on the Long Count calendar and epigraphic records. Specific events in 618 CE are not uniquely identified.
cross_references: Part of Classic Maya period (250–900 CE)

events.title: Merovingian Frankish kingdoms: Political fragmentation and regional conflicts
events.region: Western Europe (Frankish territories, modern France/Germany/Low Countries)
events.description: The Merovingian Frankish kingdoms are characterized by political fragmentation, with power divided among multiple kings and regional strongmen. The period from 600–650 CE is marked by internal conflicts, the rise of the Carolingian family (though not yet dominant), and the gradual consolidation of Frankish power. By 618 CE, the Frankish kingdoms are the dominant political force in Western Europe, though internally divided.

sources.name: Gregory of Tours, History of the Franks (6th century, but covering this period)

events.certainty_note: The broad political situation of the Frankish kingdoms in 618 CE is well-attested in Frankish chronicles, though specific events in this year are not uniquely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Sassanid capture of Antioch is disputed. Some sources place it in 613 CE, others in 618 CE. Modern scholarship is divided on the precise chronology of Sassanid advances in the early 610s CE. The founding of the Tang Dynasty in 618 CE is well-attested, but some sources give slightly different dates for the collapse of the Sui Dynasty and the consolidation of Tang power.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 618 CE is moderate. Chinese sources provide detailed accounts of the Sui-Tang transition, though these are later compilations (10th–11th centuries). Byzantine and Armenian sources document the Sassanid military campaigns. Sanskrit sources on Harsha are later compilations. Frankish sources are also later compilations. Maya epigraphic evidence is contemporary but limited to elite events. The surviving sources reflect the perspectives of Chinese imperial bureaucrats, Byzantine court historians, and Frankish nobility, with limited documentation of non-elite populations or regions outside the major empires.

graph_edges.note: Tang Dynasty founding in 618 CE leads to Li Shimin's coup and accession as Emperor Taizong in 626 CE.
Sassanid capture of Antioch in 618 CE leads to the capture of Jerusalem in 619 CE.
Tang Dynasty founding in 618 CE leads to the dynasty's rule until 907 CE.
